claim: Ben Thompson's Stratechery is the canonical paid newsletter for tech business strategy, with a Daily Update plus weekly long-form Articles, and active 2026 AI coverage including 'Aggregators and AI', 'AI Power, Now and In 100 Years', and 'AI and the Human Condition'.
quote: Stratechery by Ben Thompson – On the business, strategy, and impact of technology.

claim: Thompson continues to apply Aggregation Theory to AI in 2026, analyzing OpenAI, Anthropic and incumbents through that strategic lens.
Azeem Azhar's Exponential View has 152,000+ subscribers, ranks #7 in Substack Technology, and serves investors/founders/policymakers analyzing AI through a 3-5 year horizon.

Azhar's 2026 thesis frames AI as shifting from tools to a workforce, with the advantage going to those who can orchestrate it; covers AI coding/agentic systems, value capture, careers, energy, and the durability of the boom.
quote: 2026 will be the year when AI feels less like a set of tools and more like a workforce. The advantage will shift to those who can orchestrate it into reliable outcomes.

claim: Benedict Evans publishes a free weekly newsletter to nearly 200,000 readers, heavily concentrated in tech/media/finance, with a ~50% open rate; format is curated links plus short essays.
quote: I send an email newsletter to close to 200,000 people, once a week

claim: Evans's 2026 essays question AI hype with skeptic-but-substantive framing — e.g. 'How will OpenAI compete?' arguing OpenAI lacks unique tech, network effects or stickiness while incumbents match capability and leverage distribution.
Casey Newton's Platformer covers the intersection of tech platforms and democracy with regular AI reporting; Newton also co-hosts the Hard Fork podcast with Kevin Roose.
Newton's lens has shifted toward viewing AI as urgently disruptive and worth covering with that gravity rather than as just another product beat.
quote: Why I'm having trouble covering AI

claim: Dwarkesh Patel runs a Substack of essays alongside the Dwarkesh Podcast, with the explicit 2026 goal of moving 'from just asking questions about AI to actually helping answer them' and a blog prize for big AI questions.
In 2026 Dwarkesh launched a blog-essay competition (1,000-word limit) on big questions about AI, signaling a deliberate push to grow his written output beyond interview transcripts.
quote: Blog prize for big questions about AI

claim: Tyler Cowen released a 40,000-word AI-augmented book in March 2026 ('Rise and Decline, and the Pending AI Revolution') arguing that marginalism as an economic framework is itself approaching decline as AI begins generating breakthroughs without traditional social conditions.
quote: The Marginal Revolution: Rise and Decline, and the Pending AI Revolution

claim: Cowen continues short, high-frequency AI-economics posts on Marginal Revolution, e.g. April 2026 coverage of research showing startups with greater pre-release GenAI exposure scaled faster and accelerated through funding rounds.

Noah Smith's Noahpinion now publishes near-daily AI-heavy posts; March 2026's 'Superintelligence is already here, today' argues current systems already qualify functionally, marking a clear tonal shift from his cautious 2024 stance.

Smith explicitly updated his AI-risk views in February 2026 — still skeptical of doom scenarios but newly worried about AI-designed bioweapons — making Noahpinion a useful tracker of how a center-left economist's stance is moving.

Matt Levine's Money Stuff (Bloomberg, free with email signup) is the financial-AI lens — 2026 entries include 'Everybody Wants Some AI Debt', 'The AI Whateverpocalypse Trade', and 'AI Can Manage Your Mutual Fund', covering data-center financing and AI-related capital flows.
quote: Matt Levine - Bloomberg Opinion Columnist | Bloomberg

claim: Jack Clark's Import AI is a weekly research-focused newsletter read by ~70,000-plus subscribers; in March 2026 Clark also launched the Anthropic Institute as Anthropic's policy and governance research arm, sharpening his policy voice.

Helen Toner (CSET, Georgetown) is a primary AI-policy commentator — interim Executive Director, testified before Congress on April 22, 2026, and remains the go-to voice on China, evals and governance.
quote: Helen Toner | Center for Security and Emerging Technology

claim: Patrick McKenzie writes Bits About Money (free, roughly biweekly/monthly) on financial-infrastructure plumbing and hosts the weekly Complex Systems podcast, where 2026 episodes have included METR's research showing developers were 19% slower with AI tools while believing they were 20% faster.
quote: Bits about Money by Patrick McKenzie (patio11)

claim: Matt Rickard publishes near-daily short essays on AI strategy and engineering at blog.matt-rickard.com; 2026 themes include 'The Spec Layer', 'The Model is Not The Product' and 'Is AI a Platform Shift?' — a punchier counterpart to longer essayists.

Thomas Ptacek's 2025 Fly blog post 'My AI Skeptic Friends Are All Nuts' marked a public flip from cautious to bullish on AI coding agents; he continues writing in 2026 ('Vulnerability Research Is Cooked', April 2026) on how frontier models reshape security and dev work.
quote: My AI Skeptic Friends Are All Nuts

claim: Karen Hao's 'Empire of AI' (May 2025) won the 2026 National Book Critics Circle Award and the NYPL Helen Bernstein Award; she co-hosts BBC podcast The Interface and remains the leading critical-journalism voice on OpenAI labor, water and power, and on AGI as marketing narrative.
